channel.title: Meliorism 2.0
channel.description: Daily, research-grounded briefings for trainers, coaches, and educators. By Brian Oney (Meliorist Group).

item.title: The Opticians Chart
item.description: What the practitioner sees in the first 400 milliseconds may be a category, not a person.

item.title: The Slow Motion Review
item.description: Competence is where the problem lives. The practitioner most likely to reproduce structural power without noticing is the one who is best at reading the room.

item.title: The Calibration Register
item.description: The bias doesn't live in the rating. It lives in the framing category — and calibration rooms read for tone, not category.

item.title: The Site Survey
item.description: Rearranging the room is a learning decision. Neolithic communities already knew this.

item.title: The Confessional Booth Schematic
item.description: Brave spaces aren't built by asking people to be braver. They're built by making failure-naming structurally ordinary.

item.title: The Load-Bearing Wall
item.description: Safe spaces protect people from discomfort. Brave spaces ask people to stay in contact with it — and the difference between those two design choices determines whether the room can hold the conversation that actually needs to happen.

item.title: The Provenance Document
item.description: Before you teach a framework, know where it came from — whose field it emerged from, and what got erased in translation.

item.title: The Annotated Estimate
item.description: What you actually do is not what you are paid for. That is a structural argument, not a personal grievance — and there is a tool for it.

item.title: The Tide Pool Survey
item.description: When you stop managing a person and start actually encountering them, something shifts in the room.

item.title: The Tide Chart
item.description: When 70,000 people share one stadium, something neurological fires that no screen can replicate.

item.title: The Pressure Log
item.description: The practitioner who reads pressure changes in their nervous system — before the system becomes overwhelmed — is practicing adaptability as an instrument discipline, not an attitude shift.

item.title: The Barograph
item.description: The instrument is already running. The body logs atmospheric pressure before the mind decides what the weather means. The practitioner's work is to become a better reader of its trace.

item.title: Tradecraft
item.description: Intelligence operatives have studied situational awareness for 75 years. The practitioner who earns what they're worth sees what's actually happening in the room — before anyone says it.

item.title: Grief as Practiced Presence
item.description: Grief in a container means the container is real. The practitioner's job is not to fix it—it's to normalize it, sit with it, and expand everyone's capacity to be in its presence.

item.title: When the Metric Becomes the Target
item.description: Goodhart's Law — when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. The practitioner's trap: optimizing for scores instead of transformation.

item.title: The Switchboard Problem
item.description: Brave space facilitation works like telephone exchange infrastructure — the practitioner is the operator, not the content. Your job is to maintain the conditions that make connection across difference structurally possible.

item.title: Tradecraft
item.description: Intelligence operatives have studied situational awareness for 75 years. The practitioner who earns what they're worth sees what's actually happening in the room — before anyone says it.

item.title: The Barton Gap
item.description: Clara Barton founded the American Red Cross at 59, after decades of being underpaid for work that was unambiguously valuable. The mechanism that kept her rate low is the same one operating in your practice right now.

item.title: The Hive Deliberates
item.description: Drawing on Thomas Seeley's research on swarm decision-making, a leaderless honeybee colony can converge on a strikingly good choice — not despite having no leader, but because of how it deliberates. Your multi-generational room has the same architecture available. Most facilitators silence the dance before the quorum can form.

item.title: The Sunk Ship
item.description: Sunk cost fallacy keeps practitioners rowing a sinking boat — because turning back feels like admitting the voyage was wasted. It wasn't. It was data.

item.title: The Recency Trap
item.description: The availability heuristic makes vivid, recent events feel statistically normal. Practitioners calibrate their intuitions on the last dramatic incident — not base rates.

item.title: The Transfer Collapse
item.description: When a practitioner's financial precarity becomes the client's problem — the cognitive tax that crosses the container before either party notices.

item.title: The Stimulated Emission Problem
item.description: Facilitators are not transmitters. They are pumps. The laser teaches the mechanism: insight isn't installed — it's stimulated from what learners already carry. The gap between Einstein's theory (1917) and Maiman's working laser (1960) was a conditions problem. So is yours.

item.title: The 23-Minute Problem
item.description: You design for a learner who can focus for 45 minutes. That learner no longer exists — not by choice, but by accumulated debt.

item.title: The Story Trap
item.description: Narrative bias makes humans compress experience into stories with heroes, villains, and causes — even when reality has none. Practitioners who understand this stop fighting the story and start working with it.

item.title: How to Get Lucky
item.description: The number 13 has been feared for 700 years. Taylor Swift writes it on her hand before every show. Richard Wiseman spent 10 years studying 400 lucky people. The finding: luck is not found. It is chosen. And the choosing is a skill.

item.title: Leverage Points
item.description: Donella Meadows identified 12 places to intervene in a system. Practitioners almost always intervene at the lowest-leverage points.

item.title: Confirmation Bias
item.description: We seek evidence that confirms what we already believe. The practitioner who doesn't know this about themselves designs training that confirms their theory of the learner.

item.title: The Feynman Technique
item.description: If you can't explain it simply, you don't understand it well enough. Teaching as the deepest test of knowledge.
